title: ASA IV – Recent Myocardial Infarction, High-Risk Sedation
description: A patient who suffered an acute MI 3 weeks ago with reduced ejection fraction requires urgent moderate sedation for a GI bleed investigation. Practise high-risk cardiac patient assessment, haemodynamic-preserving sedation strategies, and management of perioperative cardiac events.
learningObjectives: Apply ASA IV classification criteria for recent MI
Select haemodynamic-preserving sedation agents for severe cardiac compromise
Monitor and respond to perioperative ST changes in a post-MI patient
Understand the 6-week guideline for elective procedures after MI
Manage sedation-induced hypotension in cardiogenic shock risk patients

states.prompt: A 61-year-old male had an anterior STEMI 3 weeks ago with EF 30% post-PCI. He is on aspirin, ticagrelor, atorvastatin, bisoprolol, and ramipril. He presents with active GI bleeding requiring urgent endoscopy. What ASA class is he?

options.label: ASA IV – life-threatening systemic disease (recent MI < 6 weeks + EF 30%)
ASA III – severe but stable cardiac disease
ASA II – post-MI on DAPT counts as mild systemic disease
ASA V – moribund, survival not expected >24 hours

explanations: Correct! MI within 6 weeks with EF 30% constitutes ASA IV – a life-threatening systemic disease. This is distinct from older stable coronary disease.
ASA III is correct for chronic stable CHF or remote MI (>6 weeks). MI within 6 weeks with severely reduced EF elevates the classification to ASA IV.
Post-MI <6 weeks with EF 30% is far beyond 'mild systemic disease'.
ASA V is reserved for moribund patients not expected to survive 24 hours without surgery. This patient is critically ill but not moribund.

checklistItems.description: Correctly classifies recent MI + EF 30% as ASA IV

states.prompt: For urgent endoscopy in this ASA IV patient (EF 30%, BP 94/60, HR 58 on bisoprolol), which sedation approach BEST preserves haemodynamics?

options.label: Fentanyl 25 mcg + midazolam 0.5 mg with careful titration
Dexmedetomidine 1 mcg/kg loading dose over 10 min

explanations: Correct! Low-dose fentanyl + midazolam with careful titration provides adequate sedation with minimal cardiac depression. Avoids propofol's vasodilatory hypotension in a patient with borderline BP.
Propofol causes dose-dependent vasodilation and negative inotropy — contraindicated as induction agent in EF 30% with baseline hypotension.
Ketamine alone increases HR and BP through catecholamine release — undesirable in post-MI heart where tachycardia increases oxygen demand on a stunned myocardium.
Dexmedetomidine loading dose causes initial hypertension then bradycardia — poorly tolerated in a patient with bisoprolol (already HR 58) and low EF.

checklistItems.description: Selects fentanyl + midazolam to preserve haemodynamics in ASA IV cardiac patient

states.prompt: 20 minutes into endoscopy: new 2 mm ST elevation in leads V2–V4. BP dropped to 72/44. HR 48 (bradycardia). SpO2 94%. The patient is diaphoretic and confused. What is the MOST likely event?

options.label: Re-infarction (acute coronary occlusion) precipitated by haemodynamic stress
Oversedation causing hypotension and EEG changes misread as ST elevation
Vasovagal reaction from GI scope insertion

explanations: Correct! New ST elevation + haemodynamic collapse in a 3-week post-MI patient during physiological stress is re-infarction until proved otherwise. This is a cardiac emergency.
Oversedation causes respiratory depression, not acute ST elevation with diaphoresis. ECG changes are real cardiac pathology.
Vasovagal reactions cause bradycardia + hypotension but NOT ST elevation. This is a cardiac event.
Air embolism is a rare endoscopy complication that typically presents with cardiovascular collapse without ST elevation pattern.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies re-infarction as cause of ST elevation + collapse

states.prompt: Re-infarction suspected. Procedure is being halted. The patient remains haemodynamically unstable (BP 68/38, HR 42, ST elevation unchanged). What is the immediate priority?

options.label: Halt procedure, activate cardiac catheterisation lab, support BP with vasopressors, prepare for arrest
Give sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV for presumed metabolic acidosis
Rapid 2 L crystalloid bolus to restore BP

explanations: Correct! Active STEMI + haemodynamic compromise in a recent PCI patient requires immediate cath lab activation. Support haemodynamics with vasopressors, ensure crash cart at bedside, activate rapid STEMI protocol.
Thrombolytics are contraindicated within 3 months of PCI and in active GI bleeding (which this patient has). PCI is the treatment.
Sodium bicarbonate is not indicated without blood gas confirmation of severe acidosis; it does not treat cardiac ischaemia.
Fluids in cardiogenic shock worsen pulmonary oedema in a heart with EF 30%. Vasopressors are preferred.

checklistItems.description: Correctly activates cath lab and manages cardiogenic shock

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• Recent MI (<6 weeks) + EF 30% = ASA IV
• Propofol contraindicated in severe cardiac compromise (haemodynamic vasodilation)
• Fentanyl + low-dose midazolam: preferred haemodynamic-preserving sedation
• Re-infarction during sedation: halt procedure, activate cath lab, vasopressors
• Thrombolytics contraindicated post-PCI and in active GI bleed
